attributes.stem:
A 59-year-old woman presents to her General Practice clinic with a two-year history of persistent, pruritic rashes on her trunk and thighs. She reports that the rashes are not responding to over-the-counter moisturisers, topical antifungal cream or 1% hydrocortisone cream. She has diet-controlled diabetes mellitus and takes no regular medications. Her last holiday was to Kenya three years prior.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Skin examination reveals multiple erythematous, scaly patches and thin plaques on her lower back, buttocks, inner thighs, breast and stomach (Image). The lesions have irregular borders and vary in size from 2 to 20 cm in diameter. There is no lymphadenopathy or hepatosplenomegaly.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next investigation?
attributes.explanation:
The most appropriate next investigation for this patient is to perform a skin biopsy. The clinical presentation of persistent, pruritic patches and plaques on non-sun-exposed areas (trunk and thighs) that are unresponsive to over-the-counter treatments is suggestive of mycosis fungoides, the most common type of primary cutaneous T-cell lymphoma. A skin biopsy is essential to confirm the diagnosis and rule out other conditions that may present similarly. Other causes of annular lesions could be tinea corporis (usually scaly and erythematous as well), pityriasis rosea (would self-resolve within three months), granuloma annulare (less likely to be pruritic and smaller in size) and sarcoidosis (tend to be more red-purple in colour and smaller in size).

A chest X-ray would not be the most appropriate initial investigation for this presentation. While it might be considered later for staging if mycosis fungoides is confirmed, it is not the best first-line investigation for diagnosing a skin condition and a skin biopsy would be done first given she has no respiratory symptoms.

Leishmania molecular testing would be used to detect leishmaniasis, a parasitic disease transmitted by sandflies called Leishmania. While the patient has a history of travel to Kenya, where leishmaniasis is endemic as 'Old World' cutaneous leishmaniasis, the clinical presentation and time course of two years are not typical for cutaneous leishmaniasis. Cutaneous leishmaniasis usually presents as a one or a few red small lesions that start as papules and evolve into 20 mm nodules or ulcers, typically on exposed areas of the body. Leishmania molecular testing is not the most appropriate first-line investigation.

Fungal microscopy and culture would be more appropriate if the clinical presentation suggested a fungal infection such as tinea corporis. Tinea corporis typically appears with scaly outer edge and central hypopigmentation, whereas tinea incognito may loose this appearance with the application of topical steroid cream. Whilst the rash in this scenario is also annular in appearance and may have a itch, there would have been some response to the antifungal application, and as thus, skin biopsy was give a more definitive diagnosis.

Patch testing is used to diagnose allergic contact dermatitis. While it can be useful in some cases of persistent rashes, the presentation described is more suggestive of mycosis fungoides than contact dermatitis, making patch testing less appropriate as the initial investigation.

Mycosis fungoides can be challenging to diagnose in its early stages as it can mimic other benign skin conditions. The rash is typically scaly and red initially, then can appear as thin, reddened areas, papules, thickened lesions or ulcers in later stages. The diagnosis is based on a combination of clinical presentation, histopathology, immunohistochemistry, and sometimes molecular studies. Once diagnosed, treatment depends on the stage of the disease and may include topical treatments, phototherapy, systemic therapies, or a combination of these approaches.
learningPoints: Mycosis fungoides is the most common type of cutaneous T-cell lymphoma, typically presenting with pruritic patches or plaques on non-sun-exposed areas.
Skin biopsy is essential for the diagnosis of mycosis fungoides, as its clinical presentation can mimic other skin conditions.
references: Hanna S, Kirchhof M. Mycosis fungoides mimicking tinea pedis. Canadian Medical Association Journal. 2016;188:17- 19.  Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5135541/
Hsu S, Le E, Khoshevis M. Differential diagnosis of annular lesions. American Family Physician. 2001:64(2):289-297. Available at: https://www.aafp.org/pubs/afp/issues/2001/0715/p289.html
Ngan V. Mycosis fungoides. In: Dermnet [online]. 2021. Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/mycosis-fungoides

attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
